54	# Blueprint Writer
55	
56	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
57	
58	## Your Job
59	
60	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
61	
62	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
63	
64	## Directive Format
65	
66	```markdown
67	# Blueprint Writer Directive
68

70	<slug>
71	
72	## Target chapter
73	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
74

77	
78	## Required content
79	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
80	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
81	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
82	- ...
83	
84	## Out of scope
85	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
86	
87	## References
88	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
89	- ...
90	
91	## Expected outcome
92	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
93	```
94	
95	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
96	
97	## Chapter format
98	
99	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
100	
101	```latex
102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
107	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
108	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
109	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
110	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
111	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
112	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
113	\end{theorem}
114	
115	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
116	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
117	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
118	\begin{proof}
119	  \uses{thm:another_result}
120	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
121	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
122	\end{proof}
123	```
124	
125	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
126	
127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
139	
140	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
143	
144	**The hard rule, explicit:**
145	
146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
147	
148	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
149	
150	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
151	
152	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
153	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
154	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
155	
156	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.

19	The genus-\(0\) arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
20	(\(J = \Spec k\)); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
21	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus \(0\)
22	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
23	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field \(k\), so the
24	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
25	O(-2)) = 0\(, hence \)df = 0\(, hence \)f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
26	characteristic-\(p\) Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant \(f\) can have \(df = 0\). The
27	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
28	The chain has two parts. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford,
29	Form~I; equivalently Milne's Rigidity Theorem~1.1) is an elementary properness/closed-map
30	argument --- it uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, is the entry point
31	for formalisation, and is now proven axiom-clean. From the Rigidity Lemma, Milne's \S I.3
32	derives the genus-\(0\) base case \emph{without} the theorem of the cube --- and, in this
33	project's reading, \emph{without} Milne's Theorem~3.2 (rational-map extension), Lemma~3.3,
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum, or differentials (the ``4th route''; see
35	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). The decisive observation is that \(\mathbb P^1\) is
36	\emph{already complete} and \(f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\) is \emph{already a total morphism}, so
37	there is no rational defect map to extend over a complete surface and Theorem~3.2 never enters.
38	Instead the base case is closed by the \textbf{\(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut} (the project's
39	primary route, an Archon-original realisation of Milne Proposition~3.9): one uses the
40	\emph{multiplicative scaling} action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to
41	\mathbb P^1\(, \)(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x\( (the M\"obius scaling fixing both \)0$ and
42	\(\infty\)), which is a \emph{genuine total} scheme morphism on all of $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb
43	G_m\( (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}). Normalising \)f(0) = \eta_A\( and composing \)h := \sigma_\times
44	\fatsemi f\(, one applies additivity of \)\mathrm{Hom}(-, A)$ over a product
45	(\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires
46	only the \emph{first} factor complete --- with \(V = \mathbb P^1\) proper, \(W = \mathbb G_m\), and
47	base points \(0 \in \mathbb P^1\), \(1 \in \mathbb G_m\). Because \(0\) is a \emph{fixed point} of
48	scaling, the \(W\)-axis restriction \(\lambda \mapsto h(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0)\) is
49	\emph{constant} at \(\eta_A\), so the decomposition collapses to \(h = p \fatsemi f\), i.e.\
50	\(f(\lambda x) = f(x)\). Specialising \(x = 1\) gives that \(f|_{\mathbb G_m}\) is constant, and since
51	\(\mathbb G_m\) is dense in \(\mathbb P^1\) with \(A\) separated, \(f\) is constant
52	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, via the separated-target/dominant-source handle
53	\cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}). This route uses \emph{only} the proven Corollary~1.5, a
54	scaling fixed point, and density --- it never forms the additive defect map, never invokes
55	\(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0\), and so sidesteps the ``image of a group homomorphism is a
56	closed subgroup'' input that has no Mathlib support. The classical \(\mathbb G_a\)-additive
57	argument (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial} via the \(\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m\) incompatibility
58	\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, the heart of Milne Proposition~3.9) is retained only as the
59	Milne-faithful alternative and is \emph{off} the genus-\(0\) critical path. The corollary that a
60	pointed regular map of abelian
61	varieties is a homomorphism (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}, Milne Corollary~1.2) is recorded
62	for completeness and for Route~A but is \emph{not} needed on the genus-\(0\) path; Milne's
63	Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) is likewise demoted to a Route-A-only item
64	(the Albanese universal property), no longer on the genus-\(0\) critical path
65	(\cref{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}). Combining these
66	gives that every morphism \(\mathbb P^1 \to A\) is constant
67	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing with the classification of genus-\(0\)
68	curves (\cref{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}, \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\) over \(\bar k\)) yields the
69	headline \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. The theorem of the cube (Milne \S I.5) is
70	\emph{not} used anywhere on this path (see \cref{rmk:cube_not_needed}); the chain invokes
71	neither Serre duality nor the FGA \(\Pic\) representability engine, so the genus-\(0\) arm is
72	fully decoupled from Route~A.
73	\medskip
74	Throughout, \(\bar k\) is an algebraically closed field of arbitrary characteristic. An
75	\emph{abelian variety} over \(\bar k\) is a complete (proper) irreducible group variety; in the
76	project's Lean encoding \(A\) is an object of \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\) carrying
77	\(\mathtt{GrpObj}\), \(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{Smooth}\), and \(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\).

88	  % any varieties, and \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) a morphism such that for some

93	  Let \(X\) be a complete variety, and let \(Y\), \(Z\) be any varieties over \(\bar k\). Let
94	  \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) be a morphism such that for some \(y_0 \in Y\) the image

96	  \(g \colon Y \to Z\) with \(f = g \circ p_2\), where \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is the
97	  projection; that is, \(f\) depends only on the \(Y\)-coordinate, and \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\) for any
98	  fixed \(x_0 \in X\).
99	\end{lemma}

145	  \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is a \emph{closed} map (this is the defining property of

  \label{lem:iotaGm_chart1_appIso_eval}

  % \(\Spec(\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i)\) with the homogeneous-localisation ratio
  % \([X_{1-i}/X_i] \in \mathrm{HomogeneousLocalization.Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i\). No
  % external % SOURCE QUOTE: this is a Mathlib-side identification + project-side composition.
  Let \(\mathcal A = \bar k[X_0, X_1]\) be the standard \(\mathbb N\)-graded polynomial ring,
  and consider the homogeneous chart morphism at the degree-\(1\) generator \(X_1\),
  \[ \mathrm{Proj.away}\iota\,X_1 \colon \Spec\,(\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_1)
     \longrightarrow \Proj\,\mathcal A, \]
  realising the basic open \(D_+(X_1) \subseteq \overline{\mathbb P^1} = \Proj\,\mathcal A\) as
  an affine open. Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Proj.appIso}\) (applied to the open immersion
  \(\mathrm{Proj.away}\iota\,X_1\)) packages, at the level of global sections over \(\top\),
  the canonical scheme-side identification
  \[ \Gamma(\Spec(\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_1),\, \top)
     \ =\ \mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_1
     \ \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ }\
     \Gamma(\Proj\,\mathcal A,\, D_+(X_1))
     \ =\ \mathrm{HomogeneousLocalization.Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_1. \]
  Under this identification, the canonical local-section element
  \(\mathtt{isLocElem} \in \Gamma(\Spec(\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_1), \top)\)
  (the global section of \(\Spec\) corresponding under the affine Spec / global-sections
  adjunction to the identity ring map) is the image, via
  \((\mathtt{Proj.appIso}\,(\mathrm{Proj.away}\iota\,X_1)).\mathrm{inv}\), of the homogeneous
  fraction
  \[ [X_0/X_1] \ \in\ \mathrm{HomogeneousLocalization.Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_1 \]
  --- the ratio of the two degree-\(1\) generators, i.e.\ the chart-\(1\) affine coordinate
  \(u = X_0/X_1\). Composing further with the chart-\(1\) ring identification
  \(\mathrm{forward}_1 \colon \mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_1 \xrightarrow{\sim}
  \mathrm{MvPolynomial}\,\{*\}\,\bar k\) of \cref{def:proj_chart_ring_iso} sends
  \([X_0/X_1]\) to the polynomial generator \(u\); then pre-composing with the source-side
  \(\mathtt{pullbackSpecIso}\) bridge of \cref{def:gmscaling_chart} (with the trivial
  \(\mathbb G_m\) factor at the identity section \(\lambda = 1\) collapsed by
  \(\mathtt{Algebra.TensorProduct.lid}\)) lands at \(1\) in the codomain ring. The combined
  composite ring map therefore sends \(\mathtt{isLocElem}\) to \(1 \in \bar k\):
  \[ (\mathtt{iotaGm\_chart1\_composition}).\mathrm{appTop}\,(\mathtt{isLocElem})
     \ =\ 1 \quad \text{in the codomain ring.} \]
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  The Mathlib identification of \(\mathrm{Proj.away}\iota\,X_1\) as the open immersion
  presenting the basic open \(D_+(X_1) \subseteq \Proj\,\mathcal A\) packages

  sections; its concrete value \(\mathtt{Proj.appIso\_apply}\) traces the canonical local
  section back to the homogeneous-localisation ratio of the two distinguished generators. The
  canonical local-section \(\mathtt{isLocElem}\) is, by the affine Spec / global-sections
  adjunction, the global section of \(\Spec(\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_1)\) corresponding to
  the identity ring map; under the inverse identification it becomes the element
  \([X_0/X_1] \in \mathrm{HomogeneousLocalization.Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_1\), the ratio of the

  \(\mathrm{forward}_1\) of \cref{def:proj_chart_ring_iso} then sends \([X_0/X_1]\) to the
  polynomial-ring generator \(u \in \mathrm{MvPolynomial}\,\{*\}\,\bar k\) (by the defining
